frameworks.name: lazy vs rich learning regime
frameworks.one_line: neural networks trade off learning speed against robustness via random vs structured hidden representations

frameworks.name: deep neural network simulation
frameworks.one_line: computational simulations define coding regimes for multitasking neural populations

frameworks.name: low-dimensional orthogonal manifold coding
frameworks.one_line: rich solution projects task representations onto orthogonal low-dimensional manifolds

frameworks.name: neural geometry of task representation
frameworks.one_line: population coding geometry compared between artificial networks and biological brains

core_question: How do neural populations code for multiple, potentially conflicting tasks, and which coding regime do biological brains use?
key_assumptions: lazy and rich learning are dissociable coding solutions to multitasking
random projections expand input dimensionality in lazy regime
rich learning privileges task-relevant over irrelevant features
neural geometry in brains can be compared to that in trained networks
manipulations: learning regime (lazy random projections vs rich structured representations)
comparison across artificial networks, human behavior/neuroimaging, and macaque PFC
key_findings: lazy learning expands input via random projections to hidden layers
rich learning yields structured representations privileging relevant features
one rich solution projects task representations onto low-dimensional orthogonal manifolds
biological brain coding patterns match the rich learning regime in dimensionality and geometry
interpretations: biological brains favor rich coding solutions trading learning speed for robustness
neural geometry of task representations bridges artificial and biological multitasking
